lesson.menu_desc: Make and refuse requests politely, and soften the message -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Handling Difficult Emails -- Making & Refusing Requests Politely
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Percival JR | Aula 17 -- Handling Difficult Emails

listenings.text: Hi Percival, this is Karen. I am reading you a difficult email before I send it, so you can tell me if it sounds polite enough. It is to the supplier, Mr. Costa, and I have to ask for two big things and refuse one of his requests. Here it is. Dear Mr. Costa, I hope you are well. I am writing to ask a small favour. I was wondering if you could possibly bring the delivery date forward by one week. I know this is short notice, so please let me know if that is a problem. There is one more thing. Would it be possible to send the updated prices by Friday? Regarding your own request, I am afraid we cannot increase the order this month, as our budget is fixed. I am sorry about that, and I do appreciate your patience. If it helps, we could review it again next quarter. Thank you very much for your understanding. Kind regards, Karen. That is the whole email. Do you think the no sounds soft enough, or is it too direct?

Hello, this is Daniel from the supplier's office, leaving a short voicemail in reply to Karen's email. Thank you for your message and for asking so politely. About the delivery date: I am afraid we cannot bring it forward by a full week, but we could possibly manage three days earlier. I hope that helps. Regarding the updated prices, certainly, I will send them by Friday, as you requested. And there is no need to apologise about the order. We completely understand that the budget is fixed, and we are happy to wait and review it next quarter. I really appreciate how clearly you explained everything. Please do not hesitate to ask if you need any other favour. Thank you again, and have a good week.

extra_audio.text: First, Karen greets the supplier warmly and says she hopes he is well. Then, she makes her first request gently, asking if he could possibly bring the delivery date forward. After that, she adds a second polite request about the updated prices. Later, she refuses his request, saying she is afraid they cannot increase the order, and softens it with a reason and an apology. Finally, she offers an alternative for next quarter and thanks him for his understanding.
